data.title: UnitedHealth: A Long-Term Compounder Worth Holding Through Any Cycle
data.description:
Following my previous article on UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH), the stock rebounded from undervalued levels and appreciated by 10%. Over roughly the past 100 days, UNH has also outperformed the benchmark by a few percentage points, and was close to my guided $400 price target. It reached $381 on Oct. 28, but then consolidated and currently trades at approximately $337.

The recent outperformance and rebound from a multi-year low appear to be promising. It signals to me that sentiment may be gradually shifting, and this also raises investor confidence. A recent Q3 2025 double-beat performance helped a lot for repricing, underlining a double-digit top-line growth. It could also signal that the majority of the concerns may already be in the rearview mirror.

I can tell already that my bullish thesis remains unchanged. I still think that for long-term investors who favor market dominance at an undervalued valuation, it may be attractive at this current price tag. At a forward P/E of about 19x versus the FY2026 EPS estimate, UNH is discounted to both peers and the benchmark, and that fuels my bullish outlook.

I still rate UnitedHealth a "Buy" with a forecast price target of $400. This reflects about 19% upside potential, which would indicate broader market outperformance. I would also like to emphasize that this estimate also doesn't consider dividend income, and a yield of 2.63% could appear as an appealing bonus to an undervalued healthcare name.

Q3 2025: Double-Beat With Double-Digit Revenue Growth

On Oct. 28, the company delivered Q3 2025 results. Finally, after three consecutive top- or bottom-line misses, the company managed to deliver a double-beat performance. With accelerated revenue growth, it may seem that UNH is prepared to extend its dominance in the space. However, the market reacted to the results with a 3.42% decline over the following trading session.

UnitedHealth posted $113.16 billion in revenue, signaling accelerated 12% growth over the past 12 months, and nearly $104 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom line arrived at $2.92 in diluted EPS, a 2.4x contraction versus $7.15 in the same quarter last year, but $0.11 above analysts' estimates. Based on the market reaction, I think this could have been a "buy the rumor, sell the news" type of event, highlighting that UNH surged from approximately $250 on Aug. 5 to $380 on the earnings day. This captures a 44% rally over a few months, and it seems natural for profit-taking to occur.

The chart above highlights an interesting correlation between the UNH stock price and top-line performance. Since 2021, it has been closely correlated. Based on the trend, the stock price was following the anticipation of top-line growth. Although revenue expansion hasn't slowed down, at the end of 2024, UNH began its correction and has been trending downward ever since.

The initial inflection point was the unfortunate event tied to the murder of the previous UnitedHealth CEO, Brian Thompson, on Dec. 4, as the stock price dropped 6% over the following trading session. Since then, the stock has collapsed, and the bearish narrative intensified. Pressure from social and broader media with viral stories about significant claim denial rates, elevated scrutiny on PBMs, and other regulatory and legal headwinds stressed the stock. There were obvious reasons behind a dramatic shift in sentiment, but the company's revenue growth remains intact. This presents business stability, resilience, and a strong moat.

Back to the report itself, in the press release, management indicated that it has experienced a drop in both earnings from operations and a margin decline. Although this was anticipated, it reflects approximately 2x contraction to $4.3 billion, and 390 bps deterioration in net margin, highlighting 2.1%. A rise in medical costs remains an ongoing issue, but I think UNH may still turnaround from the current valuation if they subside.

On the upside, UNH achieved an increase in total served customers. It has exceeded 50 million in total, with approximately 800,000 new additions in Q3 2025. The offerings tied to employer and individual experienced an addition as well, highlighting nearly 700,000 additions in self-funded, and a 200,000 increase in domestic clients.

Now, a significant downside is tied to the fact that its Optum brand is also facing bottom-line headwinds. I would like to emphasize that the deterioration in margins is across Optum operations, which are mainly driven by reductions in Medicare funding. Of course, a rise in utilization and costs also had a meaningful impact.

The good thing is that top-line growth remains intact. Although Optum has achieved an 8% revenue growth versus Q3 2024, the core segment of UNH posted more than 16% expansion in top-line, underlining $87 billion over the past three months.

Despite the bottom-line headwinds, the company's cash flow remains strong. In addition to this, the decline in diluted EPS was anticipated due to a rise in medical costs, and the market seems to have already priced this in the stock, once it collapsed to a 52-week low of $234.60. UNH is still a highly profitable healthcare giant, and once sentiment improves, it may continue its uptrend in the stock price, alongside improving fundamentals and performance.

The healthcare sector of the S&P 500 now trades at 18.47, and although it has rebounded from the recent multi-year low of about 16.50x, it has a long way to go for upside. The current environment was risk-on, and that is not favorable for the defensive sector. However, I think it is uplifting that UNH strongly rebounded from yearly lows, and if the volatility returns to the broader markets, it may reach my price target.

UnitedHealth's stock has been following top-line performance for years. The stock entered a downward channel a year ago, but after this meaningful pullback, it appears to have found a bottom and rebounded strongly from the yearly lows. I think that the majority of the headwinds may already be priced in the stock price, and it could gradually return to the correlation trend. I would also like to emphasize that it is impressive that UNH maintained a significant top-line increase despite bottom-line headwinds. Yes, the margins eroded, but it also could be close to the bottom, and the forward-looking markets may hold on to the turnaround story, reflecting an elevated premium, because of UNH's significant stance in the market.

UNH Is Still Cheap At This Price Tag, More Upside Ahead

I kindly invite you to read my previous article on UnitedHealth. It offers a more in-depth analysis of the company's valuation and also analyzes its shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach through dividends. Nevertheless, there are a few things I would like to elaborate on.

UnitedHealth trades at a forward P/E of approximately 21 versus the FY2025 EPS estimate and a 19x earnings multiple versus the FY2026 EPS expectations. I think that the 15-month forward P/E seems to be more reasonable, and I will use it below. But I would also like to flag that the chart above highlights that even a 21x earnings multiple presents a business-typical valuation. This argues that there is room for multiple expansion if steady top- and bottom-line growth continues.

Therefore, at a 19x earnings multiple, the healthcare giant trades approximately in line with peers at 19x. Versus the average company in the S&P 500 at a 23x earnings multiple, UNH remains discounted by 21%. Given the company's well-known brand, significant moat, and stable top-line performance, I think it deserves at least the benchmark's earnings multiple.

To support this, over the past 12 months, UNH achieved 10% revenue growth. This matches the performance of the average company in the S&P 500 at historical 10% revenue growth. Furthermore, UNH delivered about a 25% bottom-line increase on a year-over-year basis, and if we compare to the benchmark again, this presents a 2.5x outperformance versus recent years' 10% bottom-line growth. The concerns tied to a substantial rise in medical costs are real, but I argue that UNH remains undervalued at these levels.

Now, the short-term outlook remains uncertain, and I wouldn't be surprised that until concerns are resolved, UnitedHealth continues trading in a range. However, talks are in progress around the extension of the ACA and its subsidies, and that could heavily contribute to my bullish outlook. This also remains the highest risk for the company in the short term, and investors should monitor the outcome.

Nevertheless, I think UNH is here to stay. Its impressive resilience, highlighting a solid revenue growth despite the headwinds, argues for at least a market-like valuation. I kindly invite you to read my previous article on the company, but to sum up, UnitedHealth is still cheap at the current price tag. If tailwinds play out favorably and ACA extensions are enrolled, I would not be surprised if UNH rebounded from the current trading range.

Wall Street estimates $17.75 in diluted EPS for FY2026, reflecting a resumed bottom-line performance. I believe the company is well-positioned to achieve this target, especially if the bill passes and uncertainty fades away. If that happens, I think that investors may reprice UnitedHealth at a closer-to-market-like valuation. Thus, if we apply approximately a 22.5x earnings multiple, leaving a little bit of margin of error, to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $17.75, we arrive at my price target of $400. Time will tell, but this seems to be a reasonable target if tailwinds materialize.

My Bullish Narrative Remains, UnitedHealth Is A "Strong Buy"

My bullish narrative hasn't changed. UnitedHealth may appear compelling from different perspectives. It's a defensive stock with a dividend income. Also, the whole healthcare industry of the benchmark trades at multi-year lows. Sure, there are reasons behind suppressed valuations, but despite meaningful bottom-line headwinds, UnitedHealth delivers robust and resilient performance, indicating revenue growth.

Main risks and concerns remain, as I have indicated above, and in my previous article. Medical costs and elevated utilization remain the key concerns, but I argue that they have also been the main catalysts behind a sharp correction. The healthcare industry remains sensitive to regulatory changes, and investors should keep in mind that it could also be exposed to volatility. Also, it has a heavily leveraged capital structure, and a hawkish Fed could cloud the outlook. I would also like to emphasize that, due to its defensive origin, it may be less sensitive to macro policy and the Fed's stance, but it's important to highlight that it impacts sentiment.

However, I think UnitedHealth remains an interesting diversification opportunity. It is a market-leading player in the space that also offers a defensive approach. It could help withstand market turbulence, and yield comes as quite a nice bonus to an undervalued name. Thus, my bullish narrative remains unchanged. I still think UnitedHealth is a "Strong Buy." I forecast a $400 price target over the next 12 months, representing a 19% upside potential. In fact, 26 Wall Street analysts forecast a $392 price target, highlighting a 16% upside possibility.

data.title: The Play On ADMA Biologics: A Discounted Stock With Good Prospects
data.description:
I am circling back ADMA Biologics (ADMA) I last looked at this intriguing biotech concern in early August following Q2 results. I am revisiting ADMA Biologics as the company posted Q3 numbers and provided guidance that further confirms that management is building a bright future for shareholders as EPS is projected to quadruple by 2030. Growth that is a rarity in an overbought market. An updated analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

This New Jersey based firm finished its network of 10 plasma collection facilities after several years in 2023. From these inputs, ADMA Biologics produces two FDA approved products, BIVIGAM and ASCENIV. The stock currently trades around $20.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of just over $4.8 billion.

Third Quarter Results:

ADMA Biologics posted its Q3 numbers on November 5th. The company delivered GAAP earnings of 15 cents a share. This was in line with the consensus. GAAP net income came in at $36.4 million. This was up just one percent from the same period a year ago. However, this was largely due to a higher effective tax rate. Adjusted EBITDA rose an impressive 29% to $58.7 million.

Revenues rose 12% on a year-over-year basis to $134.2 million. This beat expectations by north of $3 million. Based on results, management upped its FY2025 sales guidance to $510 million from $500 million. FY2026 guidance was bumped to at least $630 million from $625 million. Net income projections rose significantly to $255 million in the coming fiscal year from $240 million.

In addition, ADMA Biologics continues to advance its SG-001 hyperimmune IVIG (hIVIG 10%) program. This candidate is designed to provide passive immunity against Streptococcus pneumoniae in immunocompromised patients. Management believes this could be an eventual $300 million to $500 million high margin opportunity. ADMA Biologics also has a voucher that could accelerate development and approval by two quarters.

The recently FDA approved manufacturing process change will start to be utilized in the fourth quarter. This should improve yields by 20%, which should start to significantly improved margins and profits going forward. In addition, management reaffirmed FY2029 sales guidance of at least $1.1 billion. This projection does not include any contribution from the SG-001 program, it should be noted.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Just under eight percent of the outstanding float in the shares are currently held short. The CEO of the company has sold approximately $1.7 million worth of shares since July 1st. That said, he still retains the vast majority of his stake in the firm and that has been the only insider activity in the shares in the second half of 2025.

ADMA Biologics ended the third quarter with just over $60 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet against approximately $70 million of long-term debt based on the 10-K the company filed for Q3. The company also has an undrawn on $225 million credit revolver. The company purchased $23 million of its own stock in the third quarter. Since third quarter numbers hit the wires, Cantor Fitzgerald has reissued its Buy rating and $30 price target on ADMA.

Conclusion:

The current analyst firm consensus has the company with GAAP earnings of 59 cents a share on $511 million in sales, up 19% from FY2024. They see profits rising to 91 cents a share in FY2026 on sales growth in the mid-20s.

The shares trade at just over 22 times FY2026E EPS. This a significant discount to the overall market multiple. And few firms in the S&P 500 are projected to more than quadrupled profits by FY2030 and have a likely pathway for doing so.

One month ago, when ADMA was trading at $16.00 a share, the stock was recommended exclusively to Biotech Forum members as a straight equity buy. The stock is up approximately 25% since then. Therefore, I would not add via stock purchases to my current holdings, but would if the shares drifted back to the $16 to $18 a share range.

However, there is decent liquidity and bid/ask spreads in the options against the equity. A covered call strategy can either establish an initial holding in ADMA at a significantly lower entry point or earn a nice return if the stock trades flat or rises over the option duration. And that seems the right play in ADMA stock at current prices.

data.title: RPV: Risks Point To Possible Underperformance
data.description:
The Invesco S&P 500® Pure Value ETF (RPV), launched on 03/01/2006 and managed by Invesco Capital Management LLC, provides exposure to the S&P 500 constituents that exhibit value characteristics such as low P/E, P/B, and P/S ratios. It charges an expense ratio of 0.35% and manages $1.4 billion.

While I see the value in a value approach for long-term investors, the near-term outlook suggests that value is going to underperform growth and even the broad market. RPV's recent outperformance is unlikely to persist for much longer, as the macro conditions will most likely favor different sector allocations.

Portfolio and Performance

RPV's top 10 holdings illustrate the more conservative exposure to large-cap stocks that a value bias results in. The number of holdings is relatively low at 105, but the top 10 account for 27% and the largest weight is only 3.6%.

Consistent with the value approach, Financials have the largest weight at 18.4%, as typical value metrics create a bias toward them even if they're not cheap on an absolute basis. Healthcare, though cheap compared to the index based on conventional metrics, has the advantage of being more conservative than Financials in this macro regime.

The ETF tracks the S&P 500 Pure Value Index, which doesn't just offer another value tilt but filters extreme value within the index and the number of holdings reflects this. The index, and therefore the ETF, is rebalanced on an annual basis, which is long-term enough to allow for the undervalued stocks to mean-revert or get repriced. Taking into account the long time this ETF has been active, the tracking error is negligible, showcasing very high efficiency.

Now, it's certainly interesting that RPV recently caught up to pure-growth stocks, as represented by the Invesco S&P 500 Pure Growth ETF (RPG). However, its YTD performance illustrates something very useful: even during heightened uncertainty regarding the macro conditions, growth stocks led the way.

And that's something that is likely to persist in the near term, considering the present conditions and expectations regarding key macroeconomic variables.

Macro Outlook

Inflation Expectations

Inflation expectations are hovering around 3.2%, which implies further increases from the current CPI and Core CPI levels of 3%.

This is consistent with the idea that tariffs will keep applying some upward price pressure in the short term. The thing is that many businesses might not have exhausted their pre-tariff inventories early this year when there was a surge in imports, and as they do in the near term, inflation will experience a temporary shock. As inflation expectations are partially influenced by past inflation, inflation may actually climb more than current expectations suggest; Gregory Daco, chief economist at EY-Parthenon, anticipated that tariffs should raise inflation by 0.8 percentage points by early next year.

Labor Market Conditions

The labor market is also expected to keep softening, resulting in a "stagflation lite" forecast. YTD, job growth has decelerated meaningfully, unemployment is now up again, and jobless claims have been elevated for some time, pointing to a higher unemployment rate.

Indeed, Global Investment Research estimates that U3 unemployment will climb to 4.5% this year.

Monetary Policy Path

With such an outlook on inflation and the labor market, central bank easing may be bumpy.

The FOMC December meeting concluded on Wednesday with the decision to cut by another 25 bps, the third consecutive cut this year. Also, the Fed announced that it would start to expand its Treasury portfolio on Friday after years of reducing it. However, the last decision was far from unanimous (two members opposed any cut) and Chair Powell didn't indicate any future cuts.

With stocks in the Financials sector being more sensitive to interest rate changes on average, a case for a portfolio with such a meaningful allocation to them is not easy to make. Volatility may, in fact, be higher for value ETFs with not much better returns than the broad market to show for.

This is the key risk here and it's related to opportunity cost. Sure, rates are likely to come down faster now than in the recent past, but due to sticky inflation and much less restrictive rates now, the Fed is more likely to be careful rather than stick to a strong easing narrative. Based on interest rate traders, the probability that we won't get a cut in the next FOMC meeting is now ~75%, consistent with the uncertainty that the Fed wanted to inject.

With inflation not falling rapidly towards the Fed's target and the policy path being so uncertain in the near term, broad value outperformance is not likely; Financials don't benefit in a meaningful way and cyclicals don't experience accelerated demand. Moreover, the undervaluation observed in such stocks should persist, adding another layer of opportunity risk. However, large-cap growth can perform better even during such a regime, as it is often tech-heavy due to secular narratives tied to the current winners, such as cloud and AI adoption.

Growth Trends

The economy is also expected to grow at a slower pace in the near term, at least. Real GDP growth has been quite volatile in 2025, with the first quarter marking a contraction as import growth accelerated, and then it grew by 3.8% in the second quarter, as imports materially declined. The average Real GDP growth in the first half of this year was ~1.4%, a level that, based on economic forecasts, is expected to persist in the next year.

Large-cap Growth ETFs are likely to outperform in this regime, as the largest tech stocks have narratives that make them attractive in a low-growth environment.

Conclusion

As it appears that large-cap value is likely to once again underperform the broader market and growth stocks in the short-term future, I prefer to be cautious about alternative strategies that in other regimes may have been defensive. I am rating RPV a hold for now and will keep monitoring the conditions for any opportunity that suggests a value bias will be useful for portfolios again. It's not a buy because I view growth as more attractive right now, but it's not a sell either because there is no case for it realizing losses in the near- or mid-term.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

Thank you for reading.

data.title: ARC Resources: Buy The Dip
data.description:
Introduction

We were pretty impressed with Arc Resources, (AETUF) the last time we covered them in mid-2023, giving it a buy in the $14's. The last 2.5 years have been reasonably kind to that call, with the stock grinding higher into mid-June of this year, and a peak of $22.80. It's been downhill until recently as investors were disappointed with reported production from one of their marquee projects that failed to meet expectations. As we've noted in other articles on Canadian-gas focused drillers, ARC shares have suffered by comparison, and under-performed American gas giants as well. Now we understand why.

As we've noted in numerous articles-gas is hot right at the present, encroaching winter time aside, gas has new drivers that didn't exist a few years ago. ARC's is a major Canadian gas producer with 54% of it's daily output of ~360K BOEPD in that commodity.

ARC missed forecasts for EPS and revenue for Q-3 that resulted from some voluntary curtailment's at its Sunrise core production area and a problem with wells their growth area in Attachie. This area has figured prominently in their planning over the last several years and the market was was expecting a robust performance in line with their recent forecast of 30-35K BOEPD. News that Attachie production had fallen short of this forecast by about 8K BOEPD was not well received and put a dent in the stock. The company noted in the release that things were improving at Attachie, raising expectations for Q-4.

October production at Attachie was approximately 29,000 boe per day, including approximately 13,000 barrels per day of condensate. ARC successfully drilled and completed its latest pad applying learnings from earlier in the year, which will be placed on production late in the fourth quarter of 2025.

The company plans significant growth in the next year as demand for gas and condensate-heavy oil diluent, is expected to rise. On a capital budget of $1.8-1.9 bn the company expects to deliver 2026 exit production of 405-420K BOEPD, with free funds flow of $1.5 bn CAD.

Analysts are bullish on the company, giving it a buy rating and with price targets that range from $20-$25.00. The median is $22.00 which would be about a 15% upside from current levels. I think they are being a little timid here as this just gets the company back to second quarter levels. EPS targets of $0.47 for Q-4 are a boost from Q-3's $0.29, and may help propel the stock higher into results are reported on Feb-5th, 2026.

The thesis for ARC Resources

With ARC's Montney position there is room to run for at least couple of decades without any sort of expensive M&A that could damage the balance sheet, or result in dilution to shareholders. This provides predictability for increasing shareholder returns as production ramps up. With a peer leading low cost per mcfe that is in part driven by the quality of the assets held and support processing infrastructure, they can produce profitably across the cycle.

ARC has a solid balance sheet with net debt to FFO just below 2X at current WTI, ~$60.00 and $3.5 NYMEX pricing for gas.

The company is set up to grow its dividend at a sustainable rate, that could accelerate with a declining share count. ARC's shares are priced below intrinsic value at present and the company has been retiring shares as per its NCIB. In Q-3, they bought back 6.5 mm shares and have reduced the share count by 21% over the last four years.

Q-3, 2025 and Guidance

ARC missed estimates in Q-3 substantially, which didn't help its stock as we headed into Q-4. EPS of $0.29 missed by $0.15, and revenue of $799.3 mm missed by $167.26 mm. Projections are for Q-4 EPS of $0.47, and revenues of $1.1 bn. This would be a nice sequential rebound from the Q-3 miss, and a slight shortfall from the same quarter last year. The company hedges about 20% of their 2026 production to preserve cash flow.

The company forecasts production of 405-420 BOEPD in 2026 with capex of $1.8-1.9 bn. This is about a 10% step down from 2025 and the company noted it would deliver higher production and increased free cash from 2025.

Analyzing the Attachie under lift

In the Q&A from the Q-3 call a question was asked specific to the reason for the shortfall from the 30-35K BOEPD forecast to the 25K or so realized. They were a little cagey with the answer-excess water production, but we do have a few data points that will allow me to make an educated guess as to what happened. It's important to understand this as Attachie is one of ARC's growth areas. If the underlift is not fixable with engineering, the overall investment thesis could be compromised.

So here's what we know-

  • Excess water
  • Excess clean up time
  • Refined well design
  • Well spacing

That's actually quite a bit of information, if you have some context in which to frame it. Let's realize that shale strata are laminated often between water-bearing sands. The goal of shale fracking is to position your well bore for maximum drainage once the frac is installed. The risk for well placement is encouraging water production that can leave oil stranded. If you look at the slide below it looks like that Phase I was drilled on a thinner bench than what is projected for Phase II. This amplifies the risk we've discussed in terms of well placement. Maybe the directional guy had a bad day?

One of your challenges as a well designer is to keep the bore hole in the thickest part of the reservoir- to avoid fracking into the water zone, lying just below the shale-gravity, right? Of course. And of course, doing this while also positioning it for maximum drainage-two competing objectives. I think there's a decent chance ARC fracked into water on this pad. This could be the result of drilling out of the zone, an aggressive frac treatment as well as poor well placement. In this scenario part of the well would see higher water cuts than the sections where it was avoided. This is all fixable on future wells with a refined well design, and perhaps finding a new directional guy. As they note in the slide presentation "Use learnings implemented in 2025 and 2026 to drive efficiencies."

Then there is the issue of spacing. With the wine rack or fish bone well pattern, if you don't get this right, then there could be field pressure depletion or "cross-talk" between the wells. This could impact both the cleanup and the production rates that are achieved.

The good news is that all of this is fixable with engineering and not an adverse judgement on the quality of the reservoir.

Risks

Gas prices have been selling off since topping out a few weeks ago. This could chip away at the recent upward momentum ARC stock has seen in the last few weeks. Two things affect gas prices other than the new demand coming from LNG and AI data center investments. Weather and the substitution of coal for power generation. The weather is cooperating-it's chilly out there, but when gas prices spike utilities-that can, switch to coal, and that is probably happening now.

A chilly wind is blowing through the AI buildout "boom" story. Investors are starting to ask, "where's the beef?" (Channeling a tag line from an 80's Wendy's commercial, and worrying that increasing cash flow could go to debt service rather than stock buybacks or dividends.) They worry that increased borrowing to install equipment that may become obsolete before its paid for, leaving the company with an ever-mounting debt profile. Now we are told not to panic. If a bubble is building, it's nothing like the internet bubble of 2000, these companies have real earnings and revenues. Still when the herd stampedes...you don't want to be in the way.

Your takeaway

ARC is trading at an attractive multiple as compared with peers. If you look their flowing barrel number of $27K and give them credit for projected growth, they are slightly more expensive than some. Antero Resources, (AR) trades at $24K per flowing barrel as an example. So it's a bit of a mixed bag on that measure of valuation.

There is no question gas drillers are more expensive than they were six months ago. It would be a little silly for them not to be. Gas has doubled in that time, so there is still some under performance of the equity on that measure.

ARC trades at a ~20% discount on an EV/EBITDA basis to Canadian Natural Resources, (CNQ), and Birchcliff Energy, (BIREF), that's probably induced by the problems we noted earlier. If you gave them a 7.15X multiple shares would rerate toward that $24.00 upper level picked by the analysts. I don't think that's unreasonable with the growth occurring in this market and ARC's plans to increase production.

ARC gets a buy on that basis at current levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GSK: Depemokimab And Jemperli Lead Next Wave (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the past two months, that is how much time has passed since my article "GSK's Hidden Growth Engines Beyond HIV and Oncology," GSK's shares (GSK) (GLAXF) have risen by 12.5%.

And I ask myself: What has aroused investor interest in it? Maybe strong sales of drugs/vaccines or GSK's R&D achievements?

But first of all, I would like to point out that institutional investors have increased their stake in GSK from about 400 million shares to 489.6 million, according to Fintel.

In my view, Wall Street sees that under CEO Emma Walmsley and CMO Sabine Luik, GSK is making successful steps to move beyond being just the leader in the Shingles vaccine market, as well as HIV therapeutics.

By the way, it successfully competes with Gilead's HIV-1 medications (GILD) thanks to Cabenuva [sales reached $474 million in Q3], Dovato [$923 million], and Apretude [$159 million].

It's clear that GSK is targeting, and in my opinion, this is the right strategic decision, three therapeutic areas: oncology, infectious diseases, and respiratory diseases.

So among its experimental drugs, I highlight depemokimab [CRSwNP, asthma, hypereosinophilic syndrome], GSK5764227 [small-cell lung cancer and gastrointestinal cancer], and GSK5458514 [prostate cancer].

But that's not all.

One of GSK's strategies to get closer to its European "friends", Roche Holding (RHHBY) and AstraZeneca (AZN), is to ramp up its M&A activities.

In my view, the key to these is an agreement for up to £268 million with Syndivia to develop an ADC [antibody-drug conjugate] for the treatment of mCRPC. GSK announced it on October 27.

The next day, its investors and I learned of another deal, this time with Empirico, worth up to $745 million.

In it, GSK acquired the worldwide commercial rights to EMP-012, a selective siRNA [small interfering RNA].

I think it's unnecessary to go into the details about the mechanism of action of this class of therapeutic modalities.

I'll just say that siRNA is a molecule that extremely effectively degrades specific mRNA, which ultimately prevents the synthesis of disease-causing proteins.

Empirico doesn't disclose details about the exact gene EMP-012 targets, and in GSK's press release, I only saw three words: "distinct inflammatory pathway."

But I was interested in this product candidate because it's being developed to treat patients with COPD regardless of type 2 status, as well as active smokers.

Yes, EMP-012 is currently only in a Phase 1 study.

But if its preclinical data are confirmed, it would be a severe blow to Sanofi (SNY) and Regeneron (REGN) with their itepekimab, an antibody against IL-33, which, as I mentioned earlier, failed in the AERIFY-2 Phase 3 trial [NCT04751487].

Secondly, and more importantly, GSK will find a successor to Nucala [mepolizumab], which is under pressure in the lung disease therapeutics market from Dupixent.

So, Nucala sales rose by 14.1% year-on-year to $663 million. Meanwhile, Dupixent sales, which I just recently mentioned in my article about Regeneron, reached €4.16 billion in Q3, up 19.6%.

But since I'm interested in more than just the "dry figures", I went further to find out why demand for Dupixent is growing faster than GSK's IL-5 inhibitor. And I found the answer.

SNY/REGN's anti-IL-4/IL-13 monoclonal antibody reduces moderate to severe COPD exacerbations by 30%-34% [Phase 3 BOREAS/NOTUS trials], while Nucala only lowers them by 21% [MATINEE study].

So, I finished with GSK's respiratory and HIV franchises.

And now it's time for Jemperli, which, in my opinion, will make GSK one of the leaders in the oncology market.

Demand for Jemperli is growing faster than I expected

Let me remind you that Jemperli [dostarlimab] is an anti-PD-1 antibody approved by the FDA and EMA as monotherapy and in combination with chemotherapy, carboplatin/paclitaxel, for the treatment of endometrial cancer and dMMR solid tumors.

But before I get into its sales, I wondered about the prevalence of these two types of cancer and their 5-year relative survival rates.

And first, I'll say that endometrial cancer develops from the cells in the lining of the uterus. It accounts for about 95% of all cases of uterine cancer. In turn, the five-year relative survival rate for uterine cancer is 81.1%.

But the situation with the number of cases of endometrial cancer, or as it is called in Latin, corpus uteri, disappointed me even more.

According to the WHO's Global Cancer Observatory, 676,300 women worldwide will be diagnosed with it in 2050, a 60.9% increase over 2022.

And, on dMMR solid tumors, I want to discuss a bit more, as I think not everyone is familiar with it.

These abnormal masses of tissue arise from a defective biological pathway called MMR [DNA mismatch repair].

In turn, dMMR is responsible in our bodies for correcting the DNA replication errors. That is, its task is to prevent the accumulation of mutations, or, if I say more simply, to reduce the likelihood of the formation of cancer cells.

If I look at data from pan-cancer studies, about 2.9% [from 2.7% to 3.1%] of adults with solid tumours have dMMR.

And, if I take endometrial cancer as an example, dMMR prevalence is 25% [22–28%]. For dMMR ovarian cancer, it is lower and amounts to 8% [6-11%].

Well, I have finished explaining what dMMR solid tumors and endometrial cancer are and, as promised, I will move on to discussing the sales of Jemperli.

Despite intensified competition from other PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors, AstraZeneca's Imfinzi and Merck's Keytruda (MRK), sales of Jemperli hit a new all-time high, reaching $303 million in Q3 and beating even my 'best case' scenario by $24 million.

By the way, CCO Luke Miels strengthened my belief that GSK's PD-1 inhibitor continues to occupy a leading position in the endometrial cancer market.

Moving to our growing oncology portfolio, which is up 39%. Jemperli sales were up for the 10th quarter in a row as our teams continue to differentiate Jemperli from the competition, as the only immuno-oncology medicine to demonstrate overall survival in endometrial cancer.

Jemperli's global market share in endometrial cancer is now higher than the leading competitor in DMMR.

But I want to gather more information about Jemperli.

So, according to GSK's Form 20-F, dostarlimab's U.S. and EU patents are set to expire in 2035 and 2036, respectively.

That is, I see this as a greater advantage for its investors since, currently, there are no risks of launching biosimilars to Jemperli for at least the next 10 years.

While its peak sales are expected to exceed £2 billion, CEO Emma Walmsley said in the Q2 call.

We're doing this by focusing strongly on the delivery of the 14 scale opportunities we've previously highlighted, all of them with peak-year sales above GBP 2 billion.

By the way, GSK, realizing its potential in the MSI-H/dMMR market, is actively developing it for the treatment of colon cancer [NCT05855200], head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [NCT06256588, JADE], and rectal cancer [NCT05723562, AZUR-1].

SCO Tony Wood stated on the Q3 earnings call that GSK will announce the results of these three studies between H2 2026 and 2028.

We're following that up with a study called DOMENICA, which is looking at evaluating gemperirdine a chemo-free regimen.

Importantly, as well, obviously, the rectal studies continue to progress well, where we have fantastic complete responses.

Just a quick reminder on some of those programs for you, AZUR-1, which is the locally advanced MSI-H rectal results, which we're expecting to read out in the second half of '26. AZUR-2, which is colon cancer, and there's an interim for that in '28 and the JADE study, which is in the unresectable head and neck setting for which we're also expecting readouts in '28.

So lots of momentum going around Jemperli to continue to support the growth of that medicine.

But my greatest hopes, of course, are for the use of Jemperli for the treatment of dMMR locally advanced rectal cancer.

Why? Due to the outstanding results of the Phase 2 study [NCT04165772] conducted jointly with Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center.

So, 42 patients in the dostarlimab group achieved a 100% clinical complete response rate. If this efficacy is confirmed in the AZUR-1 study, I believe Jemperli will change the treatment paradigm for dMMR/MSI-H rectal cancer.

Risks

Now, it's time to look at my table for risks.

These include RFK Jr./President Trump's information pressure on Big Pharma and weak performance of Zejula [sales of $182 million in Q3 2025 compared to $202 million in Q2] amid increased competition with Pfizer's Talzenna (PFE) and AZN's Lynparza.

Takeaway

Well, I have come to the end of the article. And I didn't forget to discuss how strong or weak GSK's numbers were in Q3.

Its revenue rose 8.6% from $10.39 billion in Q3 2024 to $11.28 billion, beating the consensus estimate by $293.4 million and my 'base case' scenario by $210 million.

GSK's non-GAAP EPS was $1.46, up 13.2% year-over-year, topping Wall Street's expectations by 19 cents or 15.4%.

Thus, Q3 showed me that GSK is thriving.

And with Blenrep [belantamab mafodotin], approved by the FDA on October 23 for the 3L+ treatment for R/R multiple myeloma, GSK's risk/benefit ratio has become even more attractive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Quanex Building Products' Surge Is Deeply Justified
data.description:
December 12th was a great day for shareholders of Quanex Building Products Corporation (NX), a company that produces and sells components that are used by OEMs in the building products space. The stock was up 8.9% in early afternoon trading after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Revenue, earnings, and adjusted earnings, all came in above what analysts anticipated.

Management mentioned that they believe even greater synergies are going to be realized associated with the purchase of Tyman plc, an international supplier of engineered fenestration components and access solutions that caters to the construction industry. We do not yet have guidance for 2026 only because management said that they want to wait until first quarter earnings. But even though there are broader economic concerns, the overall picture here looks solid. Shares are incredibly cheap, both on an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. And when you add on top of this management’s optimism, which has taken the form of additional share buybacks, it seems to me as though the Strong Buy rating I assigned the company back in July should still hold.

A great quarter, all things considered

Compared to what analysts were expecting, the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was pretty solid. Revenue for the company came in at $489.8 million. Yes, that was below the $492.2 million the company reported a year earlier. But that still meant that the company exceeded analysts’ expectations by a solid $19.1 million. Management attributed this increase primarily to the purchase of Tyman that was completed on August 1st of last year. But to really understand the performance of the company, we should touch on its operating segments.

For starters, the Hardware Solutions segment of the company saw revenue inch up from $223.7 million to $226.9 million. We also saw an increase when it came to the Custom Solutions segment, from $101.2 million to $103.4 million. The only weak spot ended up being its Extruded Solutions segment. It contracted from $180.1 million to $168.6 million. Management attributed the rise for the Hardware Solutions segment to foreign currency fluctuations and the pass-through of tariffs on to consumers. The Custom Solutions segment benefited from higher pricing that management did not link directly back to tariffs. So at least this shows potentially strong demand. However, the 6.4% drop that we saw in Extruded Solutions revenue was attributable to lower volumes.

In its earnings call, the management team of the company mentioned that there are some challenges that the company is facing. Specifically, they said that affordability is a “significant challenge,” with that being attributed to inflationary cost pressures and housing inventory shortages. These are pushing prices higher, making it more difficult for individuals to buy homes. They also think that there might be a wait-and-see component to this whereby potential homebuyers are waiting to purchase homes since they know that interest rates are only going to come down from here. And unfortunately, this kind of pressure is likely to continue into 2026 as overall demand for housing remains flat because of these conditions.

The firm also pointed out that there were some operational issues during the quarter that impacted results. At one of its window and door hardware plants in Mexico, the company had some disruptions. But management maintains that the plant in question will be fully stabilized in the first half of the 2026 fiscal year. So that's encouraging to see.

On the bottom line, we saw a drastic improvement. Earnings per share went from negative $0.30 to positive $0.43. This meant that the company exceeded analysts’ expectations by $0.23. This translated to a rise in profitability from a loss of $13.9 million to a profit of $19.6 million. On an adjusted basis, there was a small increase from $0.82 per share in profits to $0.83 per share, which also allowed the company to come in $0.31 greater than what analysts were hoping to see. But because of a decline in the number of shares, with management spending $32.4 million during the year in order to repurchase 1.71 million units, the adjusted earnings per share increase was driven only by a reduction in share count. Actual adjusted earnings declined from $38.5 million to $38 million.

Other profitability metrics for the company were mixed. We saw operating cash flow skyrocket from $5.5 million to $88.3 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, the increase is more modest, from $39.2 million to $57.2 million. However, EBITDA contracted from $81.1 million to $70.9 million. In the chart above, you can also see financial results for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. Revenue surged, as did cash flow. Profitability took a hit, but that was only because of a $302.3 million goodwill impairment, a $10.2 million charge when it came to restructuring, and increased depreciation and amortization.

I wish I could say that we had a good idea what 2026 will look like for the company. However, even management is unsure. I already mentioned their overall view of the housing market next year. Referencing that, they said that there is limited transparency and significant uncertainty regarding what the macroeconomic picture should look like. And as such, the company has decided not to provide any guidance for the 2026 fiscal year, opting instead to revisit that in the first quarter earnings release that will come out early next year.

I am of the opinion that management is bearish on the economic picture in the way that I am. As I've detailed in other articles, such as here and here, both the housing market and the economy more broadly are on shaky ground. And in all likelihood, we are heading toward a recession. The good news is that there has been some great progress when it comes to the integration of its Tyman business, which has been almost entirely integrated into the company. Initially, management expected to capture around $30 million in synergies associated with this opportunity. But now, they are pushing that expectation up to $45 million. However, we don't know the timeline for this. So there is some uncertainty as to or if this will transpire. But it does appear that somewhere between $5 million and $10 million of additional synergies will occur in 2026, though that range is dependent on volume.

Keeping in mind the significant uncertainty that exists for the 2026 fiscal year, if we use the results that management achieved for 2025 and 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are rock-bottom multiples that you rarely see unless a company is in distress. And as we looked at already, that is clearly not the case here. In addition to the stock being cheap on an absolute basis, it's also cheap compared to other similar enterprises. In the table below, I stacked it up against five such firms:

  • Apogee Enterprises, Inc. (APOG)
  • American Woodmark Corporation (AMWD)
  • Insteel Industries Inc. (IIIN)
  • Janus International Group, Inc. (JBI)
  • JELD-WEN Holding, Inc. (JELD).

And what I found was that, across the board, Quanex Building Products is the cheapest of the group.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Quanex Building Products 7.0 4.9 5.6
Apogee Enterprises 19.7 8.9 7.8
American Woodmark Corporation 13.4 8.5 8.6
Insteel Industries 15.6 23.6 8.4
Janus International Group 20.2 5.7 8.7
JELD-WEN Holding 39.2 24.8 13.3

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, I would say that Quanex Building Products is doing a fine job. Yes, there are challenges, and yes, those challenges are likely to persist in the near term. This could lead to some short-term downside for investors.

But ultimately, Quanex Building Products Corporation is a cheap company in an industry that should do well. Declining interest rates will certainly make housing more affordable. Home prices have been falling for a couple of years now. And right now, there's an estimated housing shortage of 4.7 million units. Around 8.1 million families are living with somebody that they are not related to. Once everything bottoms out and prices are normalized, this should pave the way for some upside. And it is in that context that I believe that rating it a ‘strong buy’ is the right decision.

data.title: Immunovant: 'Buy' On Competitive Batoclimab Landscape And IMVT-1402 Advancement
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Immunovant (IMVT) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Immunovant: TED Data Sets Stage For Batoclimab Regulatory Filings Going Forward". With respect to this article, I noted that the company was able to meet the primary endpoint of the phase 3 study in that patients who took batoclimab were able to achieve a 5.6 improvement change from baseline in the MG-ADL score. In addition, I noted that the company was gearing up to release results from the phase 3 studies using batoclimab to treat patients with active thyroid eye disease [TED]. As an update on this, the company was set to release data from one of the first two phase 3 studies for these patients with active TED before the end of 2025. However, due to the competitive landscape nature of this indication, it has changed its mind and is instead going to release topline results concurrently in the 1st half of 2026.

With that being said, it looks as if batoclimab was just a stepping stone to establish proof-of-concept that by blocking the neonatal Fc receptor [FcRn], it could allow for the degradation of harmful immunoglobulin G [IgG] autoantibodies. The reason why I say this is because the company has shifted its focus towards an improved anti-FcRn known as IMVT-1402. The company is well established to take IMVT-1402 towards several potential registration-enabling studies. The main focus is to use this drug to treat patients with Graves' Disease [GD] first and foremost. Speaking of which, the company just recently raised $550 million before deducting expenses, and it believes that this will be enough to fund its operations through the potential commercial launch of IMVT-1402 for the treatment of patients with GD. I had a "Strong Buy" rating based on both the development of IMVT-1402 and the expected readouts of the studies using batoclimab to treat patients with active TED.

However, with the company noting the competitive landscape of active TED, plus not putting any more focus onto this drug as an anti-FcRn, I believe it is important to bring the stock's rating down to a "Buy" rating instead. The reason to move to this rating is because there are still several catalysts for investors to keep an eye on over the next few years. For 2026, it is expected that the company is going to release results from the open-label portion of a potentially registration study of IMVT-1402 to go after difficult-to-treat rheumatoid arthritis [D2T RA] patients. Plus, a data readout from a proof-of-concept study using this very same anti-FcRn to treat patients with cutaneous lupus erythematosus [CLE]. This is just what's in store for 2026, as 2027 is expected to have topline results from three potential registration trials targeting patients with GD, Myasthenia Gravis [MG], and D2T RA.

IMVT-1402 Program Progresses Towards Superior IgG Reductions And Unmet Medical Need For Graves' Disease

As I noted above, this company has been able to establish initial clinical data with batoclimab, its first-generation anti-FcRn candidate, to treat patients with IgG-mediated disorders. Immunovant is still expected to report data from both of its phase 3 studies using this drug to treat patients with active TED. Based on the competitive nature of this indication, plus the fact that batoclimab is not the main anti-FcRn it is focusing on, it expects to concurrently release data from both of these phase 3 trials in the 1st half of 2026. More about why it shifted over to IMVT-1402 below, but the key point is that this company is already in the process of developing several late-stage studies. That is, it is already in the process of advancing this drug in several potential registration-enabling studies. For the purpose of this article, I want to emphasize its focus on developing this next-generation anti-FcRn for the treatment of patients with Graves' Disease. Before going over the two potential registration-enabling studies, plus what catalysts could come about from this entire program, I believe that it is first important to go over what GD is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Graves' Disease [GD] is characterized as an autoimmune disorder by which the immune system of a patient's body causes an overactive thyroid. The problem is that these harmful antibodies that are generated, known as Thyroid-Stimulating Immunoglobulins or TSI, attach to thyroid receptors and then mimic thyroid-stimulating hormone [TSH]. The bottom line is that an excessive amount of hormones are produced that shouldn't be. With excess hormone levels in the body, patients then experience a host of symptoms that they have to deal with as follows:

  • Increased metabolism
  • Rapid heart rate
  • Anxiety
  • Bulging eyes
  • Weight loss

This is just to name a few of the symptoms that might arise with this disorder. The global Graves' Disease market size is projected to reach $2.78 billion by 2030. This is a very large market, but it is important to note that there is a focus of what patients Immunovant is to go after with IMVT-1402. The thing to note is that the company is not going after 1st-line patients specifically. The focus for it is to go after patients who either relapse, are uncontrolled on, or intolerant to anti-thyroid drugs [ATDs]. To this end, these are the two 1st-line therapies available for these GD patients:

  • Anti-thyroid drugs or ATDs - 85% to 90%
  • Ablation - 10%

Then, 2nd-line treatment would typically look like 60% - 65% could attempt control with ATDs and then 3% to 5% with ablation. The point is that there is an unmet need of about 25% to 30% of patients who are either relapsed, uncontrolled, or intolerant to the ADTs I noted above. Further proof of this would be noted from the company [Immunovant Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #19] where it shows two pools of patients that it is targeting as follows:

  • Incident pool of patients - 20,000 U.S. 2nd-line GD patients
  • Prevalent pool of patients - 330,00 U.S relapse GD patients

This is the first crucial item to note. The other items to go over would be in terms of the mechanism of action [MOA] of IMVT-1402 and why the shift over to this drug away from batoclimab.

First and foremost, the reason for moving away from batoclimab is because it is the first-generation anti-FcRn candidate that the company was in the process of developing. In essence, this definitely had highly competitive IgG reductions for sure and definitely worked. The thing is that there was a problem with this first-generation drug, which was that it reduces albumin upon binding. Why is this bad? That's because reduce albumin leads to an increase in LDL cholesterol levels. Thus, to achieve similar IgG reductions in treating such patients but not have an effect on albumin, the company developed its next-generation anti-FcRn of IMVT-1402. Speaking of IgG reductions, the company boasts competitive ones. That is, it is possible that it has the best-in-class IgG reductions observed in studies compared to other companies. That's because both batoclimab and IMVT-1402 boast a range of IgG reductions of between 74% and 82% [Immunovant Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #6]. To observe this, these are the IgG reduction measures shown from other companies as follows:

  • Ucb RYSTIGGO [Rozanolixizumab] - 50% - 72%
  • argenx SE-ADR (ARGX) with VYVGART/VYVGART HYTRULO [Efgartigimod] - 60% - 72%
  • Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) with IMAAVY [Nipocalimab] - 61% - 64%

However, it is important to state that these figures are cross-trial comparisons, and no head-to-head studies have been done. Still, this gives an idea on the power of how Immunovant has developed their anti-FcRn to potentially be best-in-class. What's different about this is that it was developed to have a different orientation binding on the Fc receptor [FcRn]. This different form of binding leads to no effect on albumin levels and/or LDL cholesterol levels. In turn, this means IMVT-1402 is to achieve similar IgG reductions against IgG-mediated autoimmune disorders, but with an improved tolerability profile. Lastly, another reason has to do with the route of administration. That is, this anti-FcRn can be given to patients as a simple self-administered autoinjector device either at home or by a Health Care Professional [Immunovant Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #11]. With all of the reasons above, it makes sense from a competitive standpoint to move forward with IMVT-1402 and forego further development of batoclimab for the treatment of IgG-mediated autoimmune disorders.

The beauty of this company is that it is running two potential registration-enabling studies for its entire program of IMVT-1402 to treat patients with Graves' Disease [GD]. One phase 2b study, which started in December of 2024, is set to recruit adults with GD who are hyperthyroid despite being given anti-thyroid drug [ATD] treatment. The primary efficacy endpoint for this study is set to be that of the percentage of patients who are euthyroid [normal hormone level balanced] plus the values of having normal limits of T3, FT4, and Thyroid-stimulating hormone [TSH] and off ATD at week 26. The second phase 2b study is also going to recruit adult patients with GD who are hyperthyroid despite ATD therapy. The primary efficacy endpoint for this one has been set as the percentage of participants who are euthyroid and off ATD at Week 26. The thing is that this program I just went over involves the targeting of GD patients, and data readouts from the potential registration studies of using IMVT-1402 to treat patients with GD are expected in 2027. That's not all, though; other data readouts from registrational trials in the same year that are expected are for the treatment of patients with Myasthenia Gravis [MG] and difficult-to-treat rheumatoid arthritis [D2T RA].

It remains to be seen whether or not IMVT-1402 is going to be successful in treating GD patients who are hyperthyroid despite having received prior ATDs. The good news is that the company did release some additional data from a proof-of-concept study targeting such patients. However, it is important to state that the data released was for six-month [24-weeks] off-treatment uncontrolled GD patients. It is important to note that this proof-of-concept study was evaluated in two specific parts:

  • Part 1 : entailed 24-week treatment period with weeks 0-12 giving patients 680 mg of subcutaneous [SC] QW [once-weekly] batoclimab and then step-down dose of 340 mg QW SC from weeks 12-24
  • Part 2 : 24 week off-treatment follow-up period

It is important to state that 25 patients entered the first part of this study [part 1], but then only 21 patients moved on to Part 2 [24-week off-treatment period]. Upon completion of the 48-week period, it was noted that 17 out of 21 patients [80%] responded to batoclimab. What does this response even mean? It means that this group of patients had T3 and T4 levels below the ≤ upper limit of normal [ULN]. This is important because it notes the durability of response, but more importantly provides evidence of possible disease modification. Another positive finding is that for the 17 patients who maintained their T3 and T4 hormone levels, approximately 8 out of 17 of them were in ATD-free remission at the end of the 6 months after the end of batoclimab treatment. Even though this was achieved with batoclimab, it goes back to what I noted above in that IMVT-1402 has achieved similar IgG reductions but with an improved tolerability profile. Thus, the end result is the company is going to move forward with IMVT-1402 for GD and the other IgG-mediated autoimmune disorders I noted above.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Immunovant had cash and cash equivalents of $521.9 million as of September 30, 2025. The thought was that the company was going to have enough cash on hand to reach the data readout from its potential registration-enabling studies of IMVT-1402 for the treatment of patients with GD in 2027. However, it seems like the company wanted to obtain additional cash to make sure that it has enough for that key event and also possibly the commercial launch of this drug for the treatment of these GD patients. To that end, it enacted the pricing of an underwritten public offering of $550 million before deducting expenses. To this end, it is selling a total of 26.2 million shares of its common stock at $21 per share.

Another item to note is that its controlling stockholder, Roivant Sciences (ROIV) had agreed to purchase shares in this offering. With the cash it had beforehand, plus the newly raised cash from the underwritten public offering [expected to close on or about December 12, 2025], should be enough to fund its operations to the commercial launch of IMVT-1402 for GD. There is a financial tool also available to raise additional cash should it need to utilize it. This would be with respect to a sales agreement with Leerink Partners LLC, where it could offer and sell up to $150 million ATM Shares pursuant to this agreement. Thus far, no shares have been sold under this share agreement. However, it is important to mention this just in case the company chooses to tap into it in the future.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Immunovant. The first risk to consider would be with respect to the potential registration-enabling studies of IMVT-1402 for the treatment of patients with GD who are hyperthyroid despite having received ATD therapy. The company expects a major inflection point or data readouts from these studies in 2027. The risk here is that even though the first-generation anti-FcRn candidate batoclimab was able to achieve positive 6-month off-treatment data, plus prior findings of IgG reductions, there is no guarantee that similar IgG reduction and positive data will be achieved in the ongoing studies of IMVT-1402 to target these GD patients.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing evaluation of batoclimab for the treatment of patients with active thyroid eye disease [TED] in the two phase 3 studies. The company is expected to share topline results from both of these late-stage studies using this first-generation anti-FcRn candidate targeting these active TED patients in the 1st half of 2026. The risk here is that even though batoclimab continues to deliver on positive data in terms of IgG reductions in IgG-mediated autoimmune disorders, there is no assurance that this will also happen in the targeting of these specific TED patients. A negative readout of topline data from these phase 3 studies would mean a short-term decline in stock price. However, on the flip side, I don't believe it would be that bad because the company is shifting away from this indication. That is, it is highly focused on only advancing its next-generation anti-FcRn therapy IMVT-1402 for the treatment of patients with GD, which is its main program.

The third and final risk to consider would be the competitive aspect of the anti-FcRn drug development space. As I alluded to above, the company boasts potential best-in-class mean percentage IgG reductions from baseline, with a range of 74% - 82%. This range of increased IgG reductions with batoclimab and IMVT-1402 is said to possibly be superior to that of Johnson & Johnson with IMAAVY, argenx SE-ADR with VYVGART/VYVGART HYTRULO, and UCB with RYSTIGGO. The belief is that the company might have an edge with this mean percentage increase in IgG reductions observed. The risk here is that this was on the basis of cross-trial comparisons, and these can't be relied on fully. Therefore, there is no assurance that once the phase 3 trials are all said and done that IMVT-1402 is going to offer the best-in-class anti-FcRn candidate. Nor that it will be able to compete well with these other anti-FcRn drugs if it is ultimately approved by regulators.

Conclusion

Immunovant has done well to advance its two anti-FcRn candidates, batoclimab and IMVT-1402, for the treatment of patients with IgG-mediated autoimmune disorders. What remains to be seen is whether or not batoclimab will prove to work in treating patients with active TED. It is not going to be long for investors to know this outcome, because it is expected that topline results from both of the phase 3 studies using this first-generation anti-FcRn to treat them will be released in the 1st half of 2026. This is why I'm lowering my rating from a "Strong Buy" to a "Buy", because the company notes that there is a competitive landscape for TED. Also, because batoclimab isn't the key drug it is focusing on now anyways. As I have gone over above, the main focus for it is to reach topline data from its potential registration-enabling studies using IMVT-1402 to treat patients with GD. Even better, it is already in the process of running potential registration-enabling studies for this candidate targeting other indications, which are MG and D2T RA.

Expected data readouts for these are also expected in 2027 as well. Now, while 2027 is still very far away, it is encouraging at least that investors don't have to wait that long for additional results to come out from the company relating to this anti-FcRn. The reason why I state that is because it expects to release data from an open-label portion of a potential registration trial of IMVT-1402 for the treatment of patients with D2T RA. Plus, there is also another program in the works, which is the evaluation of this very same drug in a proof-of-concept study targeting patients with cutaneous lupus erythematosus [CLE].

Data from this study is expected in calendar year 2026, and this could possibly be another catalyst to boost shareholder value. With the continued development of its lead program of IMVT-1402 to treat patients with GD who are hyperthyroid despite ATD therapy, plus several ongoing registration-enabling studies using this same drug to treat patients with MG and D2T RA, I believe that this company's stock should be brought to a "Buy" rating. Again, the notion is that this name is still bullish with respect to IMVT-1402 as a next-generation anti-FcRn candidate. While batoclimab was set up to at least prove that this class of drugs can work in treating IgG-mediated autoimmune disorders. It is better that it is moving forward with a more competitive anti-FcRn candidate anyways. That is, so it can better compete against all the other FcRn drugs either approved or in clinical development.

data.title: President Trump May Reschedule Cannabis: Should You Buy MSOS Or NewLake Capital?
data.description:
Cannabis stocks are soaring on news that President Trump is looking to reschedule cannabis which would mark a major regulatory milestone for the industry. Most investors appear to be targeting the AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS), which is primarily allocated to the cannabis operators, as evidenced by their explosive moves higher. I think that this may miss the greater opportunity in NewLake Capital Partners, Inc. (NLCP), one of the landlords. The company has a net cash balance sheet, yields around 12.7%, and trades at a steep discount to both REIT peers as well as its tenants. NLCP is my top pick to invest in a potential cannabis rescheduling.

MSOS: Top Holdings

MSOS is an actively managed ETF with a reasonable 0.78% expense ratio. MSOS has concentrated its holdings to the stocks of the cannabis operators, which makes sense given its stock ticker. I note that "MSOS" stands for “multi-state operators.”

These stocks had been beaten down heavily over the last several years on account of widespread price compression in the adult-use markets.

This is evidenced by their low valuations (I note that the following chart does not incorporate Friday’s strong rally).

MSOS offers investors a liquid way to invest in the operators due to it being listed on the major exchanges. While the stocks of Trulieve Cannabis Corp. (TCNNF) and others are only listed over-the-counter, MSOS is listed on the NYSE due to it employing “total return swaps.” For most looking to invest in the operators without having to deal with harsh bid-ask spreads, MSOS is the best bet from a practical perspective.

President Trump And Cannabis Rescheduling

According to sources close to the White House, President Trump is expected to reschedule cannabis to Schedule III as soon as Monday. This may not come as a surprise to many given that cannabis is widely viewed to be a popular issue among Americans. That said, longtime investors in the space have been waiting patiently for such developments.

This is highly significant for the financials of the cannabis operators because they are currently unable to deduct operating expenses (including interest) from the calculation of taxable income, meaning that they pay income tax instead based on gross profits. In other words, many operators end up paying significant tax bills even if their operating income is minimal or even negative. Rescheduling cannabis to Schedule III would in theory remove this “280e” tax and allow these operators to pay more normal income tax rates. Take for instance the stock of TCNNF, which is the fourth largest holding in MSOS. TCNNF is known as the leader in Florida as well as having diverse operations. If rescheduling were to occur, then I estimate that its income line in the latest quarter would have transformed from a $28.8 million net loss to a $23.1 million net profit. The stock currently trades at around a $1.5 billion market cap.

While the stocks of the cannabis operators are soaring, the price movement of landlords like NLCP are more muted. I last covered NLCP in September, when I rated the stock a Strong Buy on account of the 12% dividend yield and net cash balance sheet. I noted the potential regulatory catalyst in the form of rescheduling. That catalyst finally appears to be underway, yet the stock is actually flat to down since. NLCP is a landlord for many of the well known operators such as Curaleaf (CURLF), TCNNF and Cresco Labs (CRLBF). The company is highly concentrated in both the cannabis sector as well as a small list of tenants.

The company has focused its investments in limited license states, which has thus far helped to insulate it from profitability issues at unlimited license states such as California. The four-wall coverage of its tenants stood at around 4.2x, which is notably higher than typical net lease REITs.

As discussed earlier, the current status quo is that this four-wall coverage does not mean very much due to elevated tax rates eating up much of that cash flow. A successful rescheduling may transform their cash flow models, improve their credit quality, and in my opinion form a near best-case-scenario thesis for NLCP.

In the most recent quarter, the company delivered 2.4% YoY growth in adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO") to $11 million. It is worth noting that due to employing net leases, tenants are responsible for real estate taxes, insurance, and maintenance capital expenditures. This means that FFO is very comparable with “cash available for investors.” NLCP ended the quarter with $23.6 million of cash versus just $7.6 million in debt, representing a net cash position. This is highly unusual, as net lease REITs typically employ significant leverage in the 5x to 6x EBITDA range or higher given the highly recurring cash flow stream. If cannabis really is rescheduled, then the company might feel confident to increase leverage.

On the conference call, management noted that they may see some volatility ahead. Tenant Ayr Wellness (AYRWF) has undergone a restructuring leading to the company’s properties to be “sold or wound down.” These properties accounted for around 5.9% of total rent and AYRWF is no longer paying rent. Management estimated the potential impact to be around $0.036 in AFFO per share. The company recorded $0.52 in AFFO per share in the most recent quarter. Management noted that it is their “fiduciary responsibility to evaluate all avenues for creating long-term value for shareholders and that does include considering opportunities beyond cannabis.” I suspect that this is in response to peer Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR) entering the life sciences sector. I further suspect that this diversification plan might be placed on temporary hold if rescheduling does occur.

Which Cannabis Stock Is The Best Buy?

At recent prices, NLCP stock traded at around a 12.7% yield. That is a steep discount to where other net lease REITs trade at. I view NLCP to be an ideal way to position for potential rescheduling in part due to the valuation.

We can see below that NLCP trades at a steep discount to REIT peers like Prologis (PLD) and Realty Income (O) as well as its own tenants in Curaleaf (CURLF) and Green Thumb (GTBIF). I note that regarding the latter two, the chart below does not include the roughly 30% rally in Friday’s trading (meaning NLCP’s relative discount is even larger than suggested below).

In my opinion, the landlord should command a higher relative valuation than the tenants given the stronger balance sheet and profit margins. I also note that the cannabis operators tend to have significant leverage. Between the relative valuation and the stronger balance sheet of NLCP versus MSOS's holdings, this is why I view NLCP to be a better buy than MSOS.

I can see NLCP quickly re-rating to a 10% yield or less if rescheduling goes through. That valuation would still represent a steep discount to the 5.5% to 6% yield of retail NNN REITs. NLCP’s heavy concentration to the cannabis sector would be offset by its stronger balance sheet as well as the higher same-store growth rates of cannabis leases, which hover at around 2.6% versus 0.5% to 1% for retail NNN REITs.

NLCP Stock Risks

It is possible that cannabis is not rescheduled. Investors are no stranger to disappointment on the regulatory front. Absent rescheduling, NLCP might face volatility given that its tenants continue to struggle amidst widespread price compression. While I expect the low valuation and strong balance sheet to help insulate long term downside, near term volatility could be significant. It is possible that management engages in activities that destroy shareholder value, such as diluting the stock in order to fund acquisitions. I am doubtful regarding this given this was one of the few REIT management teams to employ a share repurchase program, but it is a risk to watch out for.

NLCP Stock Conclusion

NLCP might not be as sexy as the MSOS ETF, but that is only if you ignore the fundamental characteristics such as the net cash balance sheet, high dividend yield, and relative undervaluation. The stars appear to be aligning for cannabis to finally be rescheduled, and NLCP looks like an ideal way to position ahead of that potential. NLCP is a key element of how I have framed a bullish position on cannabis rescheduling at Best of Breed Growth Stocks. I reiterate my strong buy rating.
