data.title: Silver Thursday Revisited And A Look At SILJ
data.description:
Silver soared above $65 per ounce for the first time at the week’s high, sharply eclipsing the 1980 peak. Volatility has soared with the precious metal, and today I’m taking a step back in time to revisit so-called "Silver Thursday.”

In short, the episode featured the Hunt Brothers’ massive accumulated silver position. Their attempt to corner the silver futures market occurred more than 45 years ago, but with silver now up 115% on the year, the time is right for a history lesson.

I’ll also take a fresh look at the Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF (SILJ), a fund on which I had a "Buy" rating back in August. I am downgrading the product to a "Hold," given euphoric price action and the real threat of a sharp pullback. But first, let’s profile the Hunt Brothers and their incredible silver saga.

Silver Since 1970

What Is Silver Thursday?

Silver Thursday is an infamous day in finance history. The term refers to when Nelson Bunker Hunt, William Herbert Hunt, and Lamar Hunt aimed to corner the silver futures market. Over time, spot silver and silver futures soared from under $5 per ounce in 1978 to near $50 by January 1980.

The three men, heirs to an oil fortune, built highly leveraged positions in the silver futures market. At one point, the Hunt Brothers’ silver stake was reportedly more than 30% of the world’s supply of privately held silver. Their position was in both physical silver and silver futures contracts, making it an increasingly difficult trade to manage. As time passed, the Hunts stockpiled the metal and continued buying with cash from their inheritance.

As money poured into the silver market, the price naturally soared from the mid-single digits to eventually above $40 per ounce. Amid the rally, the Hunt Brothers were too much for contrarian traders to handle, resulting in a short squeeze of historical proportions.

Trade Lesson: A short squeeze occurs when a large short position exists, often from producers hedging or speculators expecting lower prices, and rising prices force shorts to cover or face margin calls.

After procuring a commanding majority stake in the entire Comex silver futures market, their more than $4 billion position entailed just $1 billion of equity. With the spot price going parabolic, the public began to take notice. Individual investors and just everyday folks began sifting through their possessions to find any silver they could, then pawning them for profits. It was such a mainstream affair that retailer Tiffany & Co. took out a full-page ad in the New York Times to call out the Hunt Brothers’ actions.

Who were the Hunt Brothers?

The Hunt brothers were sons of Texas oil billionaire Haroldson Lafayette Hunt Jr. Upon his passing in 1974, Lafayette Hunt Jr.’s sons took their newfound fortune and invested in a handful of commodities. You see, the three heirs firmly believed in the debasement of the U.S. dollar (something many macro pundits may hold fast to in modern times). Recall that the mid-1970s through the early 1980s were marked by high inflation at home, with a falling dollar value relative to other currencies and bull markets in commodities (like oil and gold).

Precious metals indeed were among the standouts as the 70s played out, despite gold ownership being somewhat restricted in the United States at the time. The Hunts turned to silver for a hedge (and, later, intense speculation). Their accumulation did not happen overnight, though, as they built a 42-million-ounce physical position over more than six years. Their stake turned incredibly lucrative, with silver increasing 10-fold.

What Happened on Silver Thursday?

Pops and drops happen all the time in markets, and silver was a prime case in point in 1979 and early 1980. The ultimate collapse began on Jan. 7, 1980, when the Comex exchange adopted “Silver Rule 7,” which restricted positions through margin requirements on silver futures. The limit on silver futures exposure was set at 3 million ounces, and traders above that threshold had until the next month to comply. The Chicago Board of Trade also suspended the issuance of new silver contracts.

Mechanically, the Hunts’ leveraged position was weakened as lenders could no longer extend the same level of credit. Silver prices started to plunge. Once prices fell far enough, their positions breached minimum margin requirements, triggering massive margin calls.

The scramble to meet these calls culminated in Silver Thursday on March 27, 1980. Silver futures collapsed by more than 33% after the Hunt Brothers failed to meet a $100 million margin call from the Bache brokerage firm. Facing default, Bache began liquidating silver futures.

The rest was history, as spot silver dropped from the low $20s to below $10 (declining 50% over a few sessions), eventually sporting a $4-handle by 1982. The gold-silver ratio (a common precious metal gauge) fell to near 15 at silver’s peak, then soared above 100 a decade later. Today, the ratio is 67, down from 100-plus earlier this year (due to silver’s incredible 115% YTD rise).

Gold:Silver Ratio Falling Fast, Given the Silver Rally

Silver Returns by Year: 2025 Second Only to the Hunt Brothers' 1979 Rally

Impacts of Silver Thursday

Facing systemic risk, the Hunt Brothers received a bailout from a consortium of banks and companies totaling more than $1 billion. The lifeline helped cover obligations to Bache, which survived the crisis. The SEC launched an investigation into the brothers’ attempt to corner the market, and Congress even called them in for questioning. The trio were ultimately charged with manipulation.

The Hunts declared bankruptcy after being fined $134 million. Once among the wealthiest men in America, they were banned from trading commodities.

It took nearly a decade for the Hunts to unwind their silver positions and repay creditors. Silver Thursday remains a textbook example of failed risk management by traders, exchanges, and regulators. Fear and greed often drive markets... look no further than Silver Thursday and the Hunt Brothers.

A Cautionary Tale About Margin Trading

Silver Thursday underscores the importance of risk management in leveraged trading. If your position grows too large relative to your equity, small price moves can lead to ruin. That’s as true for major players like the Hunt Brothers decades ago as it is for individual investors today. Traders should always monitor their leverage and consider downside scenarios, especially during volatile markets.

At the moment, silver’s implied volatility is high at 44%. Take caution.

Silver ETF (SLV) Implied Volatility High Near 45%

ETF Profile: SILJ

Now let’s bring risk management to today by taking a fresh look at SILJ, the junior silver miner equity ETF I profiled in the summer. You can review the fundamentals, including my take on the valuation and seasonal trends, in that previous piece.

For the chart, notice in the graph below that shares put in a notable bearish reversal pattern on Friday, Dec. 12. In my view, it’s key for the fund to sustain above the October peak of $27.26, particularly on a weekly basis. I see support at the November rebound high and subsequent December low just below the $25 mark. Below that, the November low of $20.46 is a potential target if we see a protracted pullback.

With high volatility, we can’t rule out severe downside moves. But I would be surprised to see SILJ fall to where the long-term 200-day moving average is right now—close to $17. That’s where the ETF really began to take flight in Q3.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart. It printed a bearish divergence to price, as the RSI failed to make a new high while price did. That’s a bearish sign.

Overall, SILJ is at risk of falling back into the $20 to $27 range.

SILJ: Bearish Reversal Risk, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

With silver on most active investors’ radars, it’s high time for a history lesson. Silver Thursday and the story of the Hunt Brothers is an epic one in the world of investing. It’s a reminder that risk happens fast, and with a parabolic run-up in silver the commodity, there are some signs that shares of silver mining companies could consolidate after an incredible year.

data.title: CoreWeave: This Is What Real AI Scale Looks Like
data.description:
Introduction

AI data center stocks, of which the most prominent are CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS), and IREN Limited (IREN), have come under a lot of pressure in recent weeks, losing 53%, 33%, and 42% off their highs, respectively, while the S&P 500 index (SPX) is inching closer to new all-time highs. It is not unreasonable to say that investors who bought the high have been getting a bit burnt, at least for now.

Sector-wide, part of the sell-off in these names has been a result of de-risking (as these companies are still cash-burning and high beta in line with a slight broader market pullback) and profit-taking (after having rallied multiple hundreds of percent from the April lows).

Previously, I rated IREN a Strong Buy on November 18 and NBIS a Strong Buy on November 25. I have not yet covered CoreWeave, which is due for change.

Regarding CRWV, there are a few company-intrinsic issues that led to a larger selloff than the two aforementioned names, as, unlike the other two names, the stock has not surpassed its June highs in October. They had a delay in bringing capacity online with one of their providers. However, the customer agreed to extend the contract, which only delays revenue recognition.

This led to their FY25 outlook being revised and not matching the market's high expectations, though still promising rapid growth moving forward. The updated numbers stand as follows:

  • Revenue: $5.05-5.15B
  • Adjusted Operating Income: $690-720M
  • Interest Expense: $1.21-1.25B
  • Capital Expenditures: $12-14B

Previously trading at a Price/Gross profit north of 40, CoreWeave was priced to perfection, certainly leaving no room for errors like these. Naturally, this tied into the stock's decline as well.

All of this crippled investor sentiment, which in my view is a buying opportunity, as despite headwinds such as high debt and no near-term profitability, CoreWeave is a critical infrastructure provider, set to close many more crucial contracts and trades at a cheap valuation with a large revenue backlog.

Making sense of the opportunity

Compute demand growth acts as a significant tailwind for AI infrastructure providers. The global AI data center GPU market alone is projected to grow from about $10.5 billion in 2025 to roughly $77 billion by 2035, implying sustained multiyear expansion in demand for high-performance AI hardware and the facilities that host it. This corresponds to a long-term growth trajectory of roughly 22 percent compounded annually over the next decade.

Companies operating in this space portray this “hunger,” as Oracle (ORCL), Nebius, Microsoft's Azure (MSFT), Google Cloud (GOOG), and Amazon's AWS (AMZN) are all operating at or beyond their available AI GPU capacity, as confirmed by their latest quarterly results and commentary. Demand is therefore not the limiting factor, at least for now.

Further, CoreWeave scored the platinum rating from SemiAnalysis' rating of neoclouds twice in a row, which no one else has won once:

The Platinum ClusterMAX™ tier represents the highest level of recognition in the AI cloud market. According to SemiAnalysis, providers at this level “consistently excel across all evaluation criteria,” including robust security, competitive pricing, extensive technical expertise, outstanding reliability, and a seamlessly managed Slurm and Kubernetes offering.

In my view, this makes it highly likely that CRWV will be able to close further major deals and continue to aggressively grow their backlog as customers need reliability, scale, and competitive pricing.

Speaking of deals, CoreWeave recently announced a new agreement with Runway, under which Runway will use CoreWeave’s Nvidia GB300 NVL72 clusters and integrated Weights and Biases tooling to train and serve its next-generation AI video models. Financial terms were not disclosed, but it is a contract that still matters strategically because it shows that independent AI application companies are choosing CoreWeave.

CoreWeave’s growth is funded by heavy leverage and long-term leases rather than owned data centers. As of September 30, the company had $14.2B in principal debt, including term loans at 9–15%, 10% senior notes (2030/2031), a 7% revolver, 8–10% OEM financing, and a 12% Magnetar loan. Repayments are front-loaded: $0.6B is due in the rest of 2025, $3.9B in 2026, and $3.0B in 2027. Interest already weighs heavily, as Q3 net interest expense was $311M on $1.37B of revenue, and year-to-date interest reached $841M on $3.56B, roughly one-quarter of revenue. CRWV recently completed a $2.25B convertible senior notes offering due 2031 with a 1.75% coupon. This deal lowers CRWV's cash interest burden compared to its double-digit-rate debt but adds potential dilution.

Valuation

Compared to NBIS and IREN, the only other providers that secured multiple Big Tech contracts, CoreWeave trades at a significant discount based on their P/S multiple of 8.5.

Of course, high amounts of debt and a more mature business (they reported $1.4B of quarterly revenue, whereas comps AI revenues are in the low millions and are priced for more explosive growth) need to be somewhat discounted. But even looking at their CY2026 P/S multiple, they are significantly less expensive than said two peers, at:

  • CRWV 3.6
  • IREN 4.6
  • NBIS 6.8

Therefore, I do see some mean reversion as a likely outcome moving forward, as soon as sentiment around this stock improves. A major catalyst could be further large contracts signed.

Still, current valuations seem to price in no further backlog expansion and an incredibly long path to profitability, if any at all. In my view, this is too pessimistic, and CRWV will be able to outperform these expectations, making some mean reversion here likely. Even if CoreWeave retraces to their average P/S of around 15, that would bring in almost 50% of gains immediately, and even more with rising sales along with future reported quarterly results.

Risks

CoreWeave is exposed to macro risk because it is high beta, unprofitable, and highly leveraged. Its valuation is sensitive to interest rates and broader market sentiment. Interest expense already consumes roughly one quarter of revenue, which reduces financial flexibility if capital becomes more expensive or risk appetite declines.

Execution risk is the most significant. The company must bring leased data center capacity online on schedule to convert its $55.6B backlog into revenue. The above-mentioned delay and the market reaction from that show that there is no room for such error.

Customer concentration is also a central risk. CoreWeave is more reliant on OpenAI than peers NBIS and IREN. OpenAI accounts for roughly 70% of CoreWeave’s year-to-date revenue, yet OpenAI’s own financial profile shows a large gap between revenue and spending. OpenAI generated about $4.3B in revenue in the first half of 2025, yet external analysis indicates that OpenAI has committed to roughly $1T in long-term compute and infrastructure spending over the next decade, a level that far exceeds its current annual revenue. They must continue growing usage and maintain competitiveness against Gemini, Claude, and others to support these commitments.

Conclusion

Today, CoreWeave seems worth a bet as a part of an AI data center exposure in a diversified portfolio. I would not recommend more than 5% of portfolio allocation into CRWV. When the valuation becomes too stretched again, CRWV would turn into a Hold/Sell quickly, as this is mainly a multiples mean reversion play for me, as fundamentally, I favor Iren and Nebius, but that is subject to developments in macro/AI data center markets and CRWV's future financial reporting and commentary.

Rating: Initiate Buy.
